headline: Selim takes Cairo and Luther nails his theses to the door of a north German church
summary: 1517 was the year the Ottoman Selim I finished off the Mamluk sultanate at Cairo and folded the caliphate, the holy cities, and the Red Sea spice trade into his empire; the year an obscure German friar posted ninety-five Latin propositions on indulgences that would break the unity of Western Christendom; and the year Spanish ships out of Cuba first sighted the Yucatán coast, opening the road to Cortés. In Ming Beijing the pleasure-loving Zhengde emperor received Portugal's first embassy under Tomé Pires; in the western Sudan the Askia dynasty of Songhai patronised Timbuktu's scholars; in the Andes and Anahuac the last great pre-Columbian empires reached their peak in the shadow of unknown pathogens already crossing the Atlantic.

snapshots.text: The great event of 1517 in the Islamic world was the Ottoman conquest of Egypt. Sultan Selim I ('the Grim'), who had defeated the Mamluks at Marj Dabiq near Aleppo in August 1516 and killed their sultan Qansuh al-Ghawri, marched south through Syria into Egypt in the autumn. On 22 January 1517 he shattered the Mamluk field army under Tuman Bay II at Ridaniya just outside Cairo, using Ottoman firearms and artillery against the Mamluks' famed cavalry; on 30 January his troops entered Cairo. Tuman Bay led an urban resistance but was captured and hanged on 13 April at Bab Zuwayla, the great southern gate of the medieval city, ending the Mamluk sultanate that had ruled Egypt and Syria since 1250. The Abbasid shadow-caliph al-Mutawakkil III was taken to Istanbul, along with cartloads of manuscripts and Mamluk craftsmen; later tradition (largely retrospective) held he ceded the caliphate to the Ottoman sultan. Selim received the keys of Mecca and Medina from the Sharif Barakat II of Mecca, making the Ottomans the guardians of the Holy Cities. Egypt was reorganised as an Ottoman province under Hayır Bey, a former Mamluk defector; the Red Sea spice trade, silks, and grain of the Nile passed into Ottoman fiscal reach. In April the Portuguese under Lopo Soares de Albergaria unsuccessfully besieged Jeddah, part of the wider struggle over the spice routes.

On 31 October 1517, the eve of All Saints' Day, an Augustinian friar and professor of theology at the young university of Wittenberg named Martin Luther sent his Ninety-five Theses (Disputatio pro declaratione virtutis indulgentiarum) — Latin propositions for academic disputation on the abuse of indulgences — to Archbishop Albrecht of Brandenburg, who was allowing the Dominican Johann Tetzel to preach indulgences on his behalf in the neighbouring bishopric of Magdeburg to finance the rebuilding of St Peter's Basilica in Rome. Whether Luther also posted them physically on the door of the Castle Church at Wittenberg is contested, but within weeks printed copies had spread across Germany in Latin and in a German translation by Kaspar Nützel, seeded by the new printing presses of Nuremberg and Leipzig. Frederick the Wise, Elector of Saxony, had already banned indulgence sales in his territory and would shield Luther from Roman prosecution. Elsewhere in Europe, the sixteen-year-old Charles of Habsburg — already King of Castile and Aragon, and about to inherit the Holy Roman Empire from his grandfather Maximilian I in 1519 — sailed from the Netherlands to take up his Spanish thrones for the first time, landing at Villaviciosa on 19 September. In France Francis I, victor of Marignano (1515), was patronising Leonardo da Vinci at Amboise (Leonardo had arrived in late 1516). The Republic of Venice, having barely survived the War of the League of Cambrai, ratified the definitive Peace of Noyon; the Fifth Lateran Council closed in Rome on 16 March; the plague was ravaging Basel.

The Ming empire was ruled by the flamboyant and often absent Zhengde emperor (Zhu Houzhao, r. 1505-1521), who preferred hunting, foreign entertainers, and personal military escapades in a private pleasure palace called the Leopard Quarter to formal court ritual in the Forbidden City. Real power was often exercised by eunuch factions and, from 1517, by generals whom the emperor favoured. The Confucian scholar-officials led by Yang Tinghe struggled against these tendencies while the philosopher Wang Yangming (1472-1529), who in this decade was suppressing rebellions in Jiangxi and developing his influential doctrine of the innate moral knowledge of the mind (liangzhi), reshaped Neo-Confucian thought against the Cheng-Zhu orthodoxy. In June 1517 the Portuguese ambassador Tomé Pires, a apothecary and author of the Suma Oriental, arrived at Canton (Guangzhou) with a small fleet under Fernão Pires de Andrade — the first official European diplomatic mission to Ming China; the embassy would fail catastrophically over the following four years. Along the northern frontier Mongol raids by Dayan Khan continued to press the Great Wall. In Japan the Warring States (Sengoku) turmoil deepened under the impotent Ashikaga shogunate; local daimyō warlords such as the Hōjō in the Kanto, Ōuchi in western Honshu, and Shimazu in Kyushu fought for regional dominance. In Joseon Korea, King Jungjong (r. 1506-1544) presided over a Neo-Confucian reformist court dominated by the young idealist Cho Kwang-jo.

Northern India in 1517 saw a dynastic transition at Delhi. Sultan Sikandar Lodi died on 21 November 1517, and his son Ibrahim Lodi took the throne (a reign that would end at Panipat in 1526 with Babur's Mughal invasion). The Lodi state was fissured by tension between the sultan and his Afghan nobles, whom Ibrahim would alienate through a policy of centralisation. In the Rajput country the great Rana Sanga of Mewar (r. 1508-1528), ruling from Chittor, was consolidating a Rajput confederation that would soon fight the Mughals at Khanwa; in central India the Malwa sultanate at Mandu continued its slow decline. In the western Deccan the Bahmani break-up was complete: the five successor sultanates of Bijapur, Golconda, Ahmadnagar, Berar, and Bidar governed independently. In the peninsula the Vijayanagara empire under Krishnadevaraya (r. 1509-1529) — perhaps the greatest of its rulers — was at the apex of its power, having taken Raichur from Bijapur in the previous decade and patronising Telugu literature and the monumental temple architecture of Hampi. In Kabul, Zahir al-Din Muhammad Babur, descendant of Timur and Genghis, was preparing the raids into the Punjab that would culminate in his conquest of Delhi. The Portuguese Estado da Índia under Afonso de Albuquerque's successor Lopo Soares was consolidating the fortified network from Goa (taken 1510) through Calicut, Malacca (1511), and Hormuz (1515); the first Portuguese factory at Colombo had opened in 1517.

In the western Sahel the Songhai empire was in the mature phase of the reign of Askia Muhammad I (Muhammad Ture, r. 1493-1528), who had overthrown Sonni Baru a quarter-century earlier and re-oriented the state around orthodox Sunni Islamic institutions while retaining Sunni Ali's imperial reach from the Atlantic coast to Aïr and from the Sahara to the northern forest. He had performed a famously ostentatious hajj in 1496-1497, patronised the Sankore and Djinguereber mosques and the great madrasa of Timbuktu, and drawn scholars such as Ahmad Baba's teachers to Gao and Timbuktu. In the same year 1517 or nearby, Askia Muhammad was ageing, going blind, and his sons were beginning the intrigues that would depose him in 1528. In Kanem-Bornu Mai Ali Gaji was consolidating a reformed Sayfawa state around Ngazargamu. In North Africa the Ottoman conquest was reaching the Maghreb: the corsair brothers Oruç and Hızır (Barbarossa) Reis seized Algiers this very year, laying the foundations of Ottoman Algeria. In the Ethiopian highlands the Solomonic emperor Lebna Dengel (r. 1508-1540) faced the rising Adal sultanate under Mahfuz. On the Swahili coast Portuguese fortresses at Kilwa (from 1505), Sofala, and Mozambique dominated the gold and ivory trade that had once fed the Great Zimbabwe interior. Along the West African coast, the Portuguese trade in gold and enslaved people at Elmina, São Tomé, and the Kongo kingdom under Manikongo Afonso I (r. 1509-1543) — the Christian king who wrote imploring letters to King Manuel of Portugal about the ravages of the slave trade — was intensifying.

The world Europeans would call the Americas was still, in 1517, largely unaware of them, but the encounter was tightening. In February and March a Spanish slaving expedition of some 110 men in three ships out of Havana under Francisco Hernández de Córdoba sighted the northern Yucatán coast (the first Spanish contact with Maya civilisation) at Cape Catoche and, after fighting at Champotón in which more than half the crew were killed or wounded, limped back to Cuba with reports of stone cities and gold ornaments that would trigger the Grijalva voyage of 1518 and the Cortés expedition of 1519. In the Basin of Mexico the Mexica emperor Moctezuma II (r. 1502-1520) governed a mature Aztec Triple Alliance empire from Tenochtitlan, a lake city of perhaps two hundred thousand people whose Templo Mayor had been dedicated in 1487; his court was famously ritualised and his tributary reach extended across most of central Mexico. In the Andes the eleventh Sapa Inca Huayna Capac (r. c. 1493-1527) ruled Tawantinsuyu from Cuzco and from a second northern capital he was constructing at Tumipampa (Cuenca); he was campaigning to extend the empire north to modern Colombia. Smallpox, unknowingly carried by the Spanish on Hispaniola, had already begun to devastate Taíno populations in the Caribbean, whose numbers had fallen catastrophically. In the North American Mississippi valley the Cahokia paramountcy had long since fragmented; the great late-Mississippian centre of Moundville in Alabama was in decline.

In the Pacific of 1517 no European ship had yet crossed the ocean — Magellan's expedition would not enter the Pacific until late 1520 — and the great Polynesian, Melanesian, Micronesian, and Aboriginal societies continued their independent lives. In Aotearoa (New Zealand) Māori tribes (iwi) descended from the founding voyagers of the late thirteenth century had developed the classic Māori culture of kūmara horticulture, pā fortifications, elaborate carving, and rich oral tradition; local warfare among rival hapū and iwi was frequent. On Rapa Nui (Easter Island) the ahu-and-moai monumental building complex was near its apogee, with vast statues quarried at Rano Raraku transported to platforms along the coast. In the Hawaiian archipelago competing chiefdoms on each main island — Hawaii, Maui, Oʻahu, Kauaʻi — supported intensive wet-taro and dryland agriculture and complex ritual systems dedicated to Kū, Lono, and other gods. In western Polynesia the Tu'i Tonga maritime confederation continued its long-standing influence. In Australia hundreds of distinct Aboriginal nations maintained tens-of-thousands-of-years-old traditions of songline-based land management and ceremony; the north coast had a growing seasonal Makassan trepang-fishing contact from Sulawesi that may have begun around this era, though its precise start date is debated. In New Guinea highland farmers cultivated intensively drained taro, sweet potato, and sugarcane valley systems; on the coasts, Austronesian-speaking peoples sustained the exchange networks that stretched across the Massim and Bismarck seas.
